titleStmt.title: Letter from Sir Wilfrid Spender to his wife Lady Lillian Spender, 15 August 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Wilfrid Bliss Spender

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: A letter from sir Wilfrid Bliss Spender (1876-1960) to his wife, Lady Lillian Spender, née Dean, (1880-1968). In this letter, Spender expresses admiration at how Lillian remains constantly fresh when writing letters, and makes several allusions to the pet names the couple have for each other ('Brown Wolf' and 'Pink Rabbit'). Spender updates Lillian on the progress of his report, and regrets his departure from the Ulster Division, as leave has been specifically approved for that Division. He asks Lillian to send on the letter to his family.Sir Wilfrid Spender was a member of the British Army until 1913 when controversy surrounding his signature of the Ulster Solemn League and Covenant resulted in his resignation. Spender was called up for military service in 1914 and from 1915 served in the 36th (Ulster) Division. Throughout the war, Spender wrote a collection of letters to his wife Lady Lillian Spender. The letters date mainly from 1915 to 1918, when Sir Wilfrid was serving in France. The letters concern politics, the Irish question, personal life, and Wilfrid's experience of the Great War. Lady Lillian served in the Ulster Volunteer Nursing Corps during the war, and was an organiser of the Ulster Division Comforts Fund.

msIdentifier.repository: Public Record Office of Northern Ireland

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
15 Aug My pr I ought to be out, but it is raining, so
I will begin a screed though I am not as fresh as
the pr always appears to be in spite of her many
hours at the desk. I like to see the way in
which your committee think it necessary to
get your approval â even in regard to St Janes'
Palace. â I shall certainly write to you there when
I am quite sure of the address! â I am so
glad that a Wolf's paw marks has the same
effect as Cockles (?) pink pills according to the
patient, but I'm afraid the bw riffs rather
suspiciously as the compliment! I had a great wrestle this morning and
have completed the 34th page of my tome though
very far from satisfied with it. It will need a
lot of polish and I know a pr would make such
an excellent chamois leather! I hear leave has
been aproved specially for the Ulster and one or
two other divisions â boohoo why did I leave? â Today's afternoon wire caused such a lot
of head scratching â literally â I think we were
nearly an hour as I rather stuck my toes in
at one or two phrases which it were proposed. I am cogitating whether it is fair to put
in the German written remarks about the extraordin-
ary disproportion between the Saxon & Prussian troops
being employed in this battle, when I guess that
the figures may have been reversed at Verdun.
It would make have considerable effect in the
Saxon wireless stations!
At top of page 1 There is
a lot in
this letter
which ought
to have been
reserved for
a family
letter. Can't
you send it
on??
certainly the
last page
